posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Cooking the last of my Flannery Reserve New York Strips tonight — reverse sear, finished on the 92k BTU infrared searing burner — and with some help from @Robert.A.Jr, landed on the 2012 Château L’Évangile as the pairing. It was the bottle he was most excited about when I texted him the options.

One text led to another, and it turns out Robert has a 2012 L’Église-Clinet ready to go — so a 2012 Pomerol Party was born. And if we’re doing this, why not open it up to the rest of you?

2012 Bordeaux doesn’t get universal love, I know. But there are pockets of real enthusiasm here on WB, including Robert’s consistent affection for the vintage in Pomerol specifically, and threads like this one that are worth a read if you need convincing.

Anyone else sitting on a 2012 Pomerol tonight? Pull the cork and join us.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: I’ll see if I can get my 2012 La Conseillante out of my offsite this afternoon.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
ToddFrench:

Robert’s consistent affection for the vintage in Pomerol specifically, and threads like this one that are worth a read if you need convincing.

I’m in. Fun!

Vintages 2011 and 2012 are not necessarily great overall in Bordeaux, but I am a fan of both vintages in Pomerol, at least among the estates that I regularly purchase.

I’ve been eager to try this 2012 Chateau L’Eglise Clinet since our friend, Sir @William_Kelley , anointed it with a perfect 100 score, but I also surmised it needed some time. Seems like this is a perfect excuse to pop one in the sake of science and some good fun.

I’m getting back from a whirlwind business trip, just landed and driving home, I’ll decant this wine for an hour or two.

PS. For the erudite Burg fans, this is a 2012 CLEC. If you know, you know.

I actually own a couple of bottles of 2012 Château Feytit Clinet; maybe not Robert’s jam, maybe not mine–not sure exactly now how I came by them.
Unfortunately, what I need to do tomorrow morning precludes drinking one tonight–also they are in offsite.
I look forward to reading some notes.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
ToddFrench:

I’m sure you’ll be enjoying a gourmet pairing with this wine, no?

Being the hedonist and the gourmand that I am, as you well know, nothing but the finest of cuisine will cross my palate this evening. But first, being of elote body as well, I’m going cycling for an hour in prep! Every hour of pleasure requires an hour of pain.

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: No pomerol here but funny you should mention 2012 because 2012 Montrose is next up for me, a glass or 2 after this evening’s sports.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
OK, it’s time to get going!

While I don’t have Flannery steaks on hand, so that I can at least keep up with and feel the inspiration of the Toddfather French, I am whipping up some Terrine de Bœuf Réconfortante, Inspirée par le Célèbre Marché de Joseph, Servie avec Nostalgie Américaine. This should pair quite nicely with a gorgeous, maturing Pomerol like this…

IMG_27901920×2560 397 KB

Big wafts of ripe black plums, pencil shavings, dank earth and ground cover, roasted garlic. Aromas from the decanter after 1.5 hours open. Have not tried yet.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

Terrine de Bœuf Réconfortante

I would love to know more about this dish, a photo would be exceptional, and what about the package within which it came?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Come on is anyone else drinking yet? Once again I’m relegated to drinking alone. Even my life is out. Like the ninth club tennis championships of the year.

Lovely, seamless, almost lavish palate presence but with just the right amount of acid to keep the hedonism in check. Like the naughty-nice paradox. Plums, ripe black cherries with a sweet liquor note, and a big range of wet earth and dark ground cover notes. Black truffles for sure, says this little piggy. Long chewy chalky finish with sweet-ish tannins. Circling back to the nose, as it has opened up more, picking up some mint and sage as well. A bit more vanilla and oak spice than I prefer, but that’s a small nit on this young, maturing, but otherwise quite delicious wine.

Right now I’m 94 pts with upside. Will likely settle higher, but I do not see this hitting 100. I may need to circle back to the 2014, I really adored the more athletic, leaner profile on that vintage last time I tried it.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
In the decanter, getting youthful components in spades at this point - black raspberry, mocha, vanilla, and that distinct “Pomerol graphite” mixed with dried flowers. 2 hours should help this wine develop a bit - already fantastic, so silky but with tremendously balanced grip

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: I coravined a glass, 7:55pm. So may not give all it has. Will give it some time in the glass. But initial impression: black cherry and a touch of menthol possibly and spices that I cannot describe on the nose. Medium bodied, not a blockbuster at all, nice balance. Think I will wait some. No food with this, I ate earlier.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not sure about your timing. Post-2016 they are much purer and more transparent than the 2000-2015 era. They have never gone to real extremes, but the wines today are the most “Conseillante” they’ve been for a long time. Which is something one could say about quite a few properties in Bordeaux. Laporte ultimately made wines of his era; Rolland’s influence you feel in the early 2010s; and from 2016 onwards you have the Marielle-Thomas Duclos era with more refined extraction and better wood integration.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: I had the 2012 La Conseillante at lunch the other day and this particular bottle was either too young or not but neither my companion nor I rushed to finish it. It’s pretty heavy right now. I have another bottle I’ll try in a few years.
